title: Messenger RNA decapping

In [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), this 5' monophosphate is a substrate for the 5' [exonuclease](/wiki/Exonuclease "Exonuclease") Xrn1[[1]](#cite_note-1) and the mRNA is quickly destroyed. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Removal of the 5' cap structure on mRNA

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/%D0%94%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%B5%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B3%2C_%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F%2C_%D1%83_%D0%B4%D1%80%D1%96%D0%B6%D0%B4%D0%B6%D1%96%D0%B2.png/250px-%D0%94%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%B5%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B3%2C_%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F%2C_%D1%83_%D0%B4%D1%80%D1%96%D0%B6%D0%B4%D0%B6%D1%96%D0%B2.png)](/wiki/File:%D0%94%D0%B5%D0%BA%D0%B5%D0%BF%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B3,_%D0%BD%D0%B5%D0%B7%D0%B0%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%B4_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%BB%D1%8E%D0%B2%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD%D1%8F,_%D1%83_%D0%B4%D1%80%D1%96%D0%B6%D0%B4%D0%B6%D1%96%D0%B2.png)

Schematic representation of deadenylation-independent decapping in *S. cerevisiae*

The process of **[messenger RNA](/wiki/Messenger_RNA "Messenger RNA") decapping** consists of [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") of the [5' cap](/wiki/Five-prime_cap "Five-prime cap") structure on the RNA exposing a 5' monophosphate. In [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), this 5' monophosphate is a substrate for the 5' [exonuclease](/wiki/Exonuclease "Exonuclease") Xrn1[[1]](#cite_note-1) and the mRNA is quickly destroyed. There are many situations which may lead to the removal of the cap, some of which are discussed below.[[2]](#cite_note-meyer-2)

In [prokaryotes](/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote"), the initial mRNA transcript naturally possesses a 5'-triphosphate group after [bacterial transcription](/wiki/Bacterial_transcription "Bacterial transcription"); the enzyme RppH removes a [pyrophosphate](/wiki/Pyrophosphate "Pyrophosphate") molecule from the 5' end, converting the 5'-triphosphate to a 5'-monophosphate, triggering mRNA degradation by ribonucleases.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Translation and decay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA_decapping&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Translation and decay")]

Inside cells, there is a balance between the processes of [translation](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") and mRNA decay.[[2]](#cite_note-meyer-2) Messages which are being actively translated are bound by [polysomes](/wiki/Polysome "Polysome") and the eukaryotic initiation factors [eIF-4E](/wiki/EIF-4E "EIF-4E") and [eIF-4G](/wiki/EIF-4G "EIF-4G") (in eukaryotes). This blocks access to the cap by the decapping enzyme [DCP2](/wiki/DCP2 "DCP2") and protects the mRNA molecule. In nutrient-starvation conditions or viral infection, translation may be compromised and decapping is stimulated. This balance is reflected in the size and abundance of the cytoplasmic structures known as [P-bodies](/wiki/P-bodies "P-bodies").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Specific decay pathways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA_decapping&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Specific decay pathways")]

A number of specific decay pathways exist that recognize aberrant messages and promote their decapping. [Nonsense mediated decay](/wiki/Nonsense_mediated_decay "Nonsense mediated decay") recognizes premature stop codons and promotes decapping as well as decay by the [exosome](/wiki/Exosome_complex "Exosome complex"). Certain classes of [miRNA](/wiki/MicroRNA "MicroRNA") have also been shown to stimulate decapping.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Messenger_RNA_decapping&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: See also")]

* [m7G(5')pppN diphosphatase](/wiki/M7G(5%27)pppN_diphosphatase "M7G(5')pppN diphosphatase")
